title: Cat Ate a Raisin? Why It’s a Veterinary Emergency
meta_desc: If your cat ate a raisin, contact a vet immediately. Raisins can cause acute kidney failure in cats. Learn why this is a high-urgency emergency.
content_html:
TL;DR: Raisins and grapes are highly toxic to cats and can cause acute kidney failure even in small amounts. This is a medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention.

What happens to my cat if they eat a raisin or grape?

Grapes and raisins are highly toxic to felines. While the exact mechanism of toxicity is still being studied, ingestion can lead to acute renal (kidney) failure. Unlike some toxins that are dose-dependent, some cats appear to be hypersensitive to even a single raisin, making any amount a significant risk to their health.

Is my cat eating a raisin considered a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is an immediate medical emergency.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as internal kidney damage can begin before the cat shows outward signs of illness.
  • Early intervention, such as decontamination and fluid therapy, is the best way to ensure a positive outcome.
  • Contact your local veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital right away.

How does having a photo of the raisins help the veterinary triage team?

  • Show the box or a photo of the specific raisins consumed to your veterinarian to help confirm the exact product.
  • Help the medical team identify if the product was chocolate-covered or contained other toxic ingredients like xylitol.
  • Assist the team in estimating the potential toxin load.
  • Allow the triage nurse to quickly document the incident in your cat's medical record.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of grapes or raisins has resulted in acute renal failure in some dogs, and anecdotal reports exist of renal failure in cats after ingestion. The exact mechanism of toxicity is unknown, although the primary injury appears to be in the proximal renal tubular epithelium. Affected animals may develop vomiting and/or diarrhea within 6-12 hours of ingestion, along with lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared with serum urea nitrogen levels, and oliguric or anuric renal failure may develop within 24-72 hours of exposure. Prompt decontamination is recommended; emesis may be induced followed by administration of activated charcoal. Aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended with large ingestions or in cases with spontaneous vomiting and/or diarrhea. Renal function and fluid balance should be monitored during fluid administration.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: Can one raisin really kill a cat?
faqs.answer: Yes, while some cats may show no symptoms, others can develop fatal kidney failure from a single raisin. There is no known safe dose.

faqs.question: How soon will symptoms of raisin poisoning appear?
faqs.answer: Vomiting usually occurs within 6 to 12 hours of ingestion. Signs of kidney failure, such as increased thirst or lethargy, may take 24 to 72 hours to develop.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat at home unless explicitly directed by a veterinarian, as cats are highly susceptible to aspiration pneumonia and esophageal damage.
